note_data.title: Marginal and absorption costing
keyPoints: Manufacturing account is prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
Manufacturing account only includes information about the factory and actual manufacturing process
Non-production costs (administration, finance, and distribution) are recorded in the Income Statement
Cost of production = Prime cost + Factory overheads
Transfer price = Production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
Factory profit is the difference between transfer price and production cost of completed goods

definitions.definition: An account prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
The total of direct materials, direct labour, and direct expenses
Costs incurred from the running of the factory, including indirect factory wages and depreciation of factory machinery
Inventory of partly finished goods in the factory at any point in time
Percentage added to the factory cost of production to arrive at the transfer price
The production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
The difference between the transfer price and the production cost of completed goods
Profit which is not recognised until the goods are sold

howToAnswer.instruction: Show working clearly with formulas. Round to 2 decimal places unless stated otherwise.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this means that'
Show the difference in profits and explain with fixed overhead movements in stock

modelAnswer.question: Lahore Textiles Ltd has fixed costs of Rs 240,000. Variable cost per unit is Rs 15 and selling price is Rs 25. Calculate the break-even point in units and the margin of safety if budgeted sales are 30,000 units.
modelAnswer.answer:
Contribution per unit = Rs 25 - Rs 15 = Rs 10
Break-even point = Fixed costs ÷ Contribution per unit
Break-even point = Rs 240,000 ÷ Rs 10 = 24,000 units
Margin of safety = Budgeted sales - Break-even sales
Margin of safety = 30,000 - 24,000 = 6,000 units
Margin of safety % = (6,000 ÷ 30,000) × 100 = 20%
markBreakdown: Mark 4: Margin of safety units calculation
Mark 5: Margin of safety units answer

commonMistakes: Including non-production costs in the manufacturing account instead of the Income Statement
Confusing prime cost with total production cost
Not understanding the difference between transfer price and actual production cost
examinerTips: Remember that manufacturing account focuses only on factory costs and production process
Always separate direct costs (prime cost) from indirect costs (factory overheads) when calculating cost of production
formulas: Contribution per unit = Selling price per unit - Variable cost per unit
Break-even point (units) = Fixed costs ÷ Contribution per unit
Margin of safety = Budgeted sales - Break-even sales
Target profit units = (Fixed costs + Target profit) ÷ Contribution per unit
Profit under absorption costing = Profit under marginal costing + Fixed overheads absorbed in closing stock - Fixed overheads in opening stock
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Break-even chart showing total cost line, sales revenue line, break-even point intersection, fixed cost line, variable cost area, and margin of safety clearly labeled with Pakistani currency (Rs) on y-axis and units on x-axis
pastPaperRefs: M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare profit statements using both marginal and absorption costing methods and explain the difference in profit figures
O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — required calculation of unit costs under absorption costing including allocation of fixed overheads, with comparison to marginal cost per unit
M/J 2022 P1 Q15 (2 marks) — multiple choice question testing understanding of when absorption costing profit exceeds marginal costing profit

O/N 2021 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — comprehensive question requiring preparation of both costing statements, reconciliation of profit differences, and recommendation of which method to use for decision making

workedExample.question:
ABC Ltd manufactures a single product. The following information relates to the year ended 31 December 2023:
Production: 10,000 units
Sales: 8,000 units at $50 each
Direct materials: $15 per unit
Direct labour: $10 per unit
Variable overheads: $5 per unit
Fixed production overheads: $60,000
Fixed selling overheads: $20,000

Prepare profit statements using (a) marginal costing and (b) absorption costing. Explain the difference in profit. (10 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate variable cost per unit = $15 + $10 + $5 = $30 per unit
Step 2: Calculate absorption cost per unit = $30 + ($60,000 ÷ 10,000 units) = $30 + $6 = $36 per unit
Step 3: Prepare marginal costing statement: Sales $400,000, less Variable costs (8,000 × $30) $240,000 = Contribution $160,000, less Fixed costs ($60,000 + $20,000) $80,000 = Profit $80,000
Step 4: Prepare absorption costing statement: Sales $400,000, less Cost of sales (8,000 × $36) $288,000 = Gross profit $112,000, less Fixed selling overheads $20,000 = Profit $92,000
Step 5: Explain difference: Absorption costing profit is $12,000 higher because 2,000 units remain in closing stock, carrying fixed overheads of $6 per unit (2,000 × $6 = $12,000)
workedExample.answer:
Marginal costing profit: $80,000
Absorption costing profit: $92,000
Difference of $12,000 due to fixed overheads carried forward in closing stock under absorption costing
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correct variable cost calculation, 2 marks for correct absorption cost calculation, 3 marks for marginal costing statement, 2 marks for absorption costing statement, 1 mark for explaining the difference

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to include all fixed costs when calculating total fixed costs for marginal costing statements' — always include both production and non-production fixed costs in the fixed cost deduction
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently confused the cost per unit calculations, using absorption rates for marginal costing' — remember marginal cost per unit only includes variable costs
Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates could not properly explain profit differences between the two methods' — always relate profit differences to stock levels and fixed overhead absorption rates
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a comparison table showing: Left column - Marginal Costing format (Sales, less Variable costs, equals Contribution, less Fixed costs, equals Profit); Right column - Absorption Costing format (Sales, less Cost of sales, equals Gross profit, less Fixed selling/admin costs, equals Profit). Include arrows showing how fixed production overheads are treated differently - expensed immediately in marginal costing vs. absorbed into stock valuation in absorption costing.
note_data.quickRecap: Manufacturing accounts calculate production costs by adding factory overheads to prime cost. Only factory-related costs are included, while administration and distribution costs go to the Income Statement. Transfer pricing involves adding a mark-up to production costs, creating manufacturing profit that remains unrealised until goods are sold.
